topic.title: HTML Form Validation and Accessibility
topic.description: Constraint attributes and ARIA error patterns make HTML forms validate and stay accessible to all.

content.title: Client-Side Validation Cannot Replace Server-Side Validation
content.text: Browser constraint validation can be bypassed entirely: users can disable JavaScript, submit via curl, or remove the required attribute with DevTools. Client-side validation improves UX by giving fast feedback without a round-trip. Server-side validation enforces correctness and security. Both are always required — they have different jobs.

examples.title: Constraint Validation, ValidityState, and Custom Error Messages
examples.description: This example shows all ValidityState flags in a live form, setCustomValidity for custom error messages, the Constraint Validation API's checkValidity and reportValidity methods, preventing form submission until all fields are valid, and the difference between native browser validation bubbles and custom accessible error messages.
examples.code_snippet:
Constraint Validation Reference

1. ValidityState flag explorer

Live ValidityState inspector
required, type="number", min=1, max=10, step=2
valueMissing: —
typeMismatch: —
patternMismatch: —
rangeUnderflow: —
rangeOverflow: —
stepMismatch: —
tooShort: —
tooLong: —
valid: —

2. setCustomValidity for server-side async errors

Username availability check
3-20 chars: letters, numbers, underscores.

3. novalidate with full custom validation loop

document.querySelector('form').addEventListener('submit', function(e) {
  e.preventDefault();
  let firstInvalid = null;

  this.querySelectorAll('input, select, textarea').forEach(el => {
    // Clear prior custom errors before re-validating
    el.setCustomValidity('');

    if (!el.checkValidity()) {
      const err = document.getElementById(el.id + '-err');
      const v = el.validity;

      // Build human-readable message from ValidityState flags
      let msg = el.validationMessage; // browser default
      if (v.valueMissing)    msg = el.labels[0]?.textContent + ' is required.';
      if (v.typeMismatch)    msg = 'Please enter a valid ' + el.type + '.';
      if (v.patternMismatch) msg = el.title || 'Invalid format.';
      if (v.tooShort)        msg = 'At least ' + el.minLength + ' characters required.';
      if (v.rangeUnderflow)  msg = 'Minimum value is ' + el.min + '.';
      if (v.rangeOverflow)   msg = 'Maximum value is ' + el.max + '.';

      el.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'true');
      if (err) { err.textContent = msg; err.classList.add('visible'); }
      if (!firstInvalid) firstInvalid = el;
    } else {
      el.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'false');
      const err = document.getElementById(el.id + '-err');
      if (err) err.classList.remove('visible');
    }
  });

  // Move focus to first invalid field for screen reader users
  if (firstInvalid) {
    firstInvalid.focus();
  } else {
    this.submit(); // All valid: allow submission
  }
});
examples.output: Page renders three sections. The ValidityState explorer shows a number input with constraints and a two-column grid of flag chips that update in real time as the user types — false flags shown in green, true in red. The username section shows a text input with a Check availability button that runs async validation and displays either a red taken error or a green available message. The novalidate section shows a dark code block with a complete custom validation loop including human-readable error building and focus management.

examples.title: Accessible Error Patterns: aria-describedby, aria-invalid, and role=alert
examples.description: This example demonstrates the complete accessible error pattern for a production registration form: aria-describedby linking hint and error text to inputs, aria-invalid toggling on validation failure, role=alert for immediate screen reader announcement, focus management after failed submission, a live region summary of all errors, and the difference between role=alert and aria-live='polite'.
examples.code_snippet:
Accessible Form Error Patterns

Please fix the following errors:

    Your name as it appears on your ID.
    At least 8 characters.
    Must be 13 or older.

    Aria pattern reference

    <!-- 1. Link hint AND error to input -->
<input aria-describedby="hint-id error-id">
<span id="hint-id">Hint text</span>
<span id="error-id" role="alert">Error text</span>

<!-- 2. Mark field invalid on error -->
input.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'true');   // error
input.setAttribute('aria-invalid', 'false');  // cleared

<!-- 3. Immediate announcement: role="alert" -->
<span role="alert">This announces immediately.</span>

<!-- 4. Polite announcement: aria-live="polite" -->
<span aria-live="polite">Waits for screen reader to finish.</span>

<!-- 5. Error summary: role="alert" + focus() -->
<div role="alert" aria-atomic="true" tabindex="-1" id="summary">
  <h3>Please fix these errors:</h3>
  <ul>...</ul>
</div>
<script>
  // After populating, focus the summary
  document.getElementById('summary').focus();
</script>
    role="alert" vs aria-live="polite":
    role="alert" interrupts the screen reader immediately (assertive). Use for validation errors that must be acted on.
    aria-live="polite" waits until the reader finishes the current sentence. Use for status messages and search counts.
examples.output: Page renders two sections and a reference block. The accessible form shows a red error summary panel (hidden initially) above four fields. On submit with empty fields, the summary expands listing all errors, the summary receives focus, each field gains a red border and an inline error prefixed with a warning icon. The aria patterns reference shows a dark code block with five patterns. The annotation card explains the difference between role=alert and aria-live=polite.

subtopics.title: Form Validation Attributes (required, pattern, min, max)
subtopics.description: required, pattern, min, max, and step define rules; the browser checks ValidityState on submit.

text.value: HTML constraint attributes declare rules that the browser enforces before a form is submitted.
(no surrounding slashes, anchored to the full value) and sets
attribute provides the browser's native validation tooltip message for

rows: Anchored to full value; use title= for error text
Browser also prevents entry beyond limit

examples.title: Validation Attributes in Practice with Custom Error Messages
examples.description: This example shows every constraint attribute in a realistic context: required with aria-required, pattern with title for custom browser tooltip, min/max on date and number, minlength with live character counter, step on number, and setCustomValidity for overriding the browser's default error message with user-friendly text.
examples.code_snippet:
Form Validation Attributes

required
Submit empty to see required error.
pattern
pattern="[a-zA-Z0-9_]{3,20}" — full value must match.
type="tel" + pattern: dial-pad keyboard + format validation.
min and max
min=13 max=120. Under-13: rangeUnderflow.
min/max dates in YYYY-MM-DD format.
step=5: stepMismatch fires for 3, 7, 11...
minlength and maxlength
0 / 160 minlength=20: tooShort if fewer than 20 chars on submit. maxlength=160: hard limit; browser blocks further typing.

Custom validation messages with setCustomValidity

// Replace browser's generic message with user-friendly text
const inp = document.getElementById('v-age');

inp.addEventListener('change', function() {
  // Always clear first to reset customError flag
  this.setCustomValidity('');

  if (this.validity.rangeUnderflow) {
    this.setCustomValidity(
      'You must be at least 13 years old to register.'
    );
  } else if (this.validity.rangeOverflow) {
    this.setCustomValidity(
      'Please enter a realistic age (maximum 120).'
    );
  }
  // If setCustomValidity('') is not called at the start,
  // old messages persist even after the value is corrected.
});
examples.output: Page renders four fieldsets and a code block. The required fieldset shows a name input that gains a red border if submitted empty. The pattern fieldset shows username with 3-20 char regex and phone with tel pattern, both turning red on invalid input. The min/max fieldset shows age (13-120), a date picker with range, and a price field with step=5. The minlength fieldset shows a bio input with a live character counter that turns red below 20 chars. Clicking Validate all triggers all browser constraint checks. The code block shows setCustomValidity with clear-first pattern.

subtopics.title: Datalist Element Usage and Autocomplete Attributes
subtopics.description: datalist adds filtered type-ahead suggestions; autocomplete enables browser-stored autofill.

text.value: elements whose values become the suggestion list for any
. The browser renders a native dropdown filtered by the user's typed characters. Unlike
, the user may type any value — the suggestions are advisory, not mandatory.
attribute (not to be confused with datalist) tells the browser and password managers the
of the field, enabling autofill from stored data. It satisfies

examples.title: datalist Patterns: Text, Email, Range, and Color Suggestions
examples.description: This example shows datalist with a text input for filtered city suggestions, datalist with email for recent recipients, datalist with range for labelled tick marks, datalist with color for a palette picker, a dynamic datalist populated via fetch, and the important distinction between datalist (advisory suggestions, free text allowed) and select (mandatory choice from fixed list).
examples.code_snippet:
Datalist Element and Autocomplete

1. Filtered text suggestions

Suggestions filtered as you type. Any value accepted.

2. Email with recent recipients

Suggestions come from datalist, not from autocomplete.

3. Range slider with labelled tick marks

Standard
datalist adds tick marks. label attribute shows in Firefox.

4. Color input with brand palette

datalist provides preset brand swatches in the picker.

5. Populating datalist dynamically

<input type="text" id="product" list="product-list">
<datalist id="product-list"></datalist>

<script>
  document.getElementById('product')
    .addEventListener('input', async function() {
      if (this.value.length < 2) return;

      // Fetch suggestions from API
      const res = await fetch(
        '/api/products?q=' + encodeURIComponent(this.value)
      );
      const items = await res.json(); // ["Widget A","Widget B"]

      const dl = document.getElementById('product-list');
      dl.innerHTML = items
        .map(name => '<option value="' + name + '">')
        .join('');
    });
</script>

6. datalist vs select

Featuredatalistselect
Free text entry Yes — any value accepted No — must choose from list
Filtered suggestions Yes — filtered as user types No — shows full list always
Custom value valid Yes No
Best for Suggestions with free entry Fixed mandatory choices
autocomplete vs datalist:
autocomplete="email" tells the browser to fill from its stored addresses (profile data, password manager).
list="recipients" provides app-specific suggestions from a <datalist> you control.
Both can be present on the same input simultaneously.
examples.output: Page renders six sections. City shows a text input where typing filters Indian city suggestions from the datalist dropdown. Email shows an input with four named recipient suggestions. Range shows a slider with datalist tick marks and a live label updating to Slow/Standard/Ultra. Color shows a picker where the datalist adds brand colour swatches to the native picker palette. Dynamic shows a dark code block with fetch-powered datalist population. The comparison table shows four rows contrasting datalist and select. The annotation card explains autocomplete vs datalist.

text.value: , not constraints. The browser filters them as the user types but does not prevent submission of values outside the list. If you need to enforce that the user selects one of a fixed set of values, use
or pair the input with a
attribute that validates against the list of allowed values. The
provides a description shown alongside the value in Firefox; other browsers may display only the value.
signals the browser to populate the field from its
from data you define. Setting both on one input means the browser may offer both its own autofill suggestions and the app's datalist suggestions in the dropdown.
event for live search-as-you-type suggestions. Clear and repopulate

elements on each response. This gives the UX of a custom autocomplete widget — filtered server-side suggestions — with zero accessibility overhead because the browser handles keyboard navigation and screen reader announcements natively.

subtopics.title: Accessible Form Design and HTML ContentEditable Attribute
subtopics.description: aria-invalid marks invalid fields; role=alert announces errors; contenteditable enables editing.

text.value: Accessible form design requires three parallel concerns. First,
and its error message linked via
so screen readers announce the error immediately after the label. Second,
: errors injected into the DOM after page load need
: after a failed submission, focus must move to the first invalid field or an error summary so keyboard users know what to fix. The
attribute makes any element editable but is
: its content is not submitted by
— it must be read via JavaScript. Reference:

rows: Screen reader reads error after label
Waits for reader to finish current sentence
Brings keyboard users to the error area
Makes any element a live editable area

content.title: contenteditable Is Not a Form Control
content.text: A contenteditable element has no name attribute and is not included in form serialisation. Its content must be read via element.textContent or element.innerHTML in JavaScript before submitting. It also has no built-in required or pattern validation. Use it only for rich text editors (WYSIWYG). For simple multi-line text input, use <textarea> which is a proper form control.

examples.title: Accessible Error Patterns, Focus Management, and contenteditable
examples.description: This example shows the complete accessible form error pattern: aria-describedby linking hint and error text, aria-invalid toggling, role=alert on inline errors, an error summary with tabindex=-1 and focus management, the polite vs assertive live region comparison, and a contenteditable rich text editor with role=textbox that collects content via JavaScript before form submission.
examples.code_snippet:
Accessible Forms and contenteditable

1. Full accessible error pattern

Please correct these errors:

    We will send your confirmation here.
    At least 8 characters.

    2. contenteditable rich text editor

    contenteditable vs textarea: Use contenteditable only for rich text (bold, italic, lists). For plain multi-line text, use <textarea> — it is a real form control with built-in validation and serialisation.
    Bio (rich text)
    0 / 500 chars
    Tell us about yourself. Supports bold, italic, and lists.

    3. role="alert" vs aria-live="polite"

    <!-- Assertive: interrupts screen reader immediately -->
<span role="alert">
  Password is required.
</span>

<!-- Polite: waits for reader to finish current utterance -->
<span aria-live="polite">
  Showing 42 results.
</span>

<!-- aria-atomic="true": re-reads the entire region on update -->
<!-- aria-atomic="false": reads only the changed portion     -->

<!-- Trigger announcement by changing textContent: -->
span.textContent = 'New message'; // fires immediately for alert
                                  // or politely for aria-live
    When to use each:
    role="alert" for validation errors — the user must act now.
    aria-live="polite" for search counts, character counters, autosave status — informational, not urgent.
    Never use role="alert" for updates that fire frequently (e.g. every keystroke) — it becomes overwhelming.
examples.output: Page renders three sections. The accessible form shows an error summary panel and two fields. On submit with empty fields the summary panel expands with a bullet list of errors, receives focus, and each field gains a red border with an inline error prefixed by an exclamation mark. The contenteditable section shows a rich text editor with Bold, Italic, and List buttons, a live character counter, and a Save bio button that validates for emptiness, sets aria-invalid, announces the error via role=alert, and logs the payload. The live region section shows a dark code block followed by an annotation card.
